Q1: Honestly, I didn't really have a study routine. I'm 29, working part-time, and juggling a lot of things, so studying happened whenever I could squeeze it in. Most of the time I'd tell myself I'd study later, and then later never really came. When I did sit down, I'd scroll through materials without a real plan, just trying to get through something. It was chaotic and I knew it wasn't working, but I couldn't seem to change it.
Q2: My pattern was all over the place. I'd go days without touching anything course-related, then panic when a deadline was close and try to cram. Sometimes work would pick up or I'd have family stuff, and the course just fell to the bottom of the list. There was no consistency at all, which I think made everything harder in the end.
Q3: Usually panic about a deadline or getting an email reminder that something was due. Sometimes I'd log in just to feel like I was doing something productive, even if I ended up just clicking around. The CBT sessions I was doing made me try to set intentions to log in more regularly, but the follow-through was weak. Boredom with the content made it hard to motivate myself otherwise.
Q4: Not really, no. I tried a few times to make it a habit, like logging in every morning with my coffee, but it never stuck. The interface felt like a chore and the content didn't grab me. I'd log in, feel overwhelmed by how much I'd missed, and then close the tab. It never became natural or automatic for me.
Q5: The discussion forums were actually kind of useful when I participated because hearing other perspectives helped things click. Short video summaries were good too since I could watch them on my phone during breaks at work. Anything hands-on or practical felt more relevant to me because I learn better by doing than by reading walls of text.
Q6: I ignored most of the long reading materials and the detailed lecture notes. They were just too dense and I'd zone out after a few paragraphs. The supplementary resources felt like extras I couldn't afford time for. I kept telling myself I'd get to them eventually, but I never did.
Q7: If I could see how something connected to real life or my future career, it felt worth doing. Activities that were interactive or had immediate feedback kept my attention better. But honestly, a lot of the time nothing felt worth it because I was so checked out and bored. The anxiety made me want to engage, but the boredom made everything feel pointless.
Q8: Required activities felt like obligations hanging over my head, which stressed me out but at least got me to attempt them. Optional stuff? I barely even looked at it. I figured if I was already struggling with the required work, why add more to my plate. The optional activities felt like they were for students who had their lives together, not me.
Q9: Yeah, I downloaded a bunch of PDFs and videos early on thinking I'd study offline. I'd read some things on my phone during my commute or at work when it was slow. I also looked up YouTube videos explaining concepts because sometimes outside sources explained things better. None of that would show up in the system.
Q10: There were times I'd sit with printed notes or downloaded materials and actually try to understand something, but I wasn't logged in. I'd work through problems on paper or rewatch videos I'd saved. The LMS wouldn't know I spent two hours struggling with a concept because I wasn't clicking around online while doing it.
Q11: Definitely. I'd download lecture slides or readings once, then work from those files for weeks without going back to the LMS. It was easier to access them offline, especially when my internet was unreliable at home. So my one download turned into multiple study sessions that the system never tracked.
Q12: Around midterms everything fell apart. Work got busier, my anxiety spiked, and I just couldn't focus on anything academic. The CBT sessions helped me recognize I was spiraling, but knowing it didn't stop it. I felt so far behind that catching up seemed impossible, so I kind of gave up for a while.
Q13: Absolutely. When I was exhausted from work or stressed about money, the LMS was the last thing I wanted to deal with. Sometimes I'd be so mentally drained that I'd log in, stare at the screen, and log out without doing anything meaningful. Competing responsibilities always won over coursework because they had more immediate consequences.
Q14: All the time. I'd have tabs open, videos playing, but my mind was somewhere else entirely. I'd realize I'd been staring at the same page for twenty minutes without absorbing anything. The anxiety made me feel like I should be doing something, but the boredom and fatigue meant nothing actually stuck.
Q15: I postponed assignments constantly even when I knew they'd pile up. Part of it was anxiety making me avoid things that felt overwhelming. Part of it was just not caring enough because the material bored me. I'd tell myself I worked better under pressure, but really I was just procrastinating out of dread.
Q16: Deadlines were basically the only thing that got me to do anything. I'd ignore the course for weeks, then frantically try to submit something at the last minute. The deadline panic was the only motivation strong enough to cut through my disengagement. It wasn't healthy, but it was the only system I had.
Q17: When I actually got feedback, it sometimes motivated me to try harder on the next thing. But feedback often came too late or felt generic, so it didn't always help. After a while, the negative feedback just confirmed what I already felt—that I was failing and maybe not cut out for this. It became demoralizing rather than helpful.
Q18: The course structure felt rigid and didn't accommodate different life situations. Everything was scheduled assuming students had tons of free time, which I didn't. The lack of flexibility made it harder to participate on my own terms. I needed more self-paced options, but the structure pushed me further into disengagement.
Q19: The overwhelming amount of content made engagement harder. When I logged in and saw dozens of unread items, I'd shut down. Shorter modules or clearer priorities would have helped. The discussion forums were good for engagement, but they couldn't compensate for how lost I felt with everything else.
Q20: That sounds like me, honestly. I logged in a lot, clicked around, maybe started things but never finished them. High activity doesn't mean productive activity. Sometimes you're just anxious and checking things repeatedly without actually accomplishing anything. The system counts clicks, not comprehension.
Q21: I can see how that happens. Some people are efficient—they get what they need quickly and study offline. They don't need to be constantly logged in to learn. My low activity didn't lead to good performance, but I know people who barely touched the LMS and did fine because they had better study methods elsewhere.
Q22: Logs can show when you logged in and what you clicked, but they can't show if you understood anything or if you were actually paying attention. They miss all the offline studying, the mental struggles, the life circumstances affecting your engagement. They're like measuring someone's fitness by counting how often they enter a gym, not what they do inside.
Q23: Being engaged means actually caring about what you're learning and feeling connected to it. It's not just going through the motions but being mentally present and curious. For me, engagement would mean not feeling bored and anxious every time I opened the course. It's about wanting to learn, not just having to.
Q24: The LMS wouldn't show how hard I was fighting my own mind just to attempt anything. It wouldn't show the anxiety attacks before assignments or the hours spent feeling guilty about not studying. It can't capture that I wanted to succeed but felt trapped by boredom, exhaustion, and circumstances. The data shows failure, but not the struggle behind it.
